Based on the detailed review of the evidence gathered about the nerve supply to the angle of the jaw, I confirm that the primary source of sensory innervation to this region indeed derives from the mandibular division (V3) of the trigeminal nerve. This finding reinforces our prior conclusions and highlights how specifically structured neurological pathways ensure precise functional control over areas like the angle of the jaw. The evidence underscores the intricate nature of human neuroanatomy, detailing various branches and connections emanating from V3 that contribute to complex tasks such as chewing and speech articulation, with particular relevance noted around structures close to the mandibular foramen and associated nerves.
Given this reaffirmation supported by extensive anatomical descriptions and clinical considerations highlighted in the literature reviewed, I concur entirely with the initial determination. Therefore, my answer remains unchanged:
